sections.text: Welcome to Biology, Module One — The Chemistry of Life. Before we talk about cells, organs, or ecosystems, we need the rule that quietly runs all of them. Every living thing is built from atoms, water, and four big molecules. Today you learn how chemistry becomes biology.

Open your hand. Skin, muscle, blood — all of it is just atoms in clever arrangements. About ninety six percent of your body is only four elements: carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen. Life is not a separate magic substance. It is chemistry that has learned how to keep itself going.

Game plan. First, atoms and the bonds that hold them together. Second, why water is not just background liquid. Third, the four macromolecules — carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, nucleic acids — each tied to its monomer and job. Fourth, how cells build and break those polymers. Two pauses, one trap, one recap.

Atoms join through bonds. Covalent bonds share electrons and hold molecules together — that is what links carbons in a sugar. Ionic bonds give electrons away and form salts like sodium chloride. Hydrogen bonds are weak attractions between molecules — small on their own, but together they shape water, DNA, and proteins.

Water is not just the puddle around the chemistry. Its molecule is polar — slightly negative on the oxygen, slightly positive on the hydrogens. That polarity lets water dissolve nutrients, stick to itself, climb up plant stems, and stabilize body temperature. Without water's polarity, your cells could not move materials at all.

Four macromolecules run living systems. Carbohydrates store quick energy — monomer is a monosaccharide like glucose. Lipids store long energy and build membranes — built from fatty acids and glycerol. Proteins do almost every job — monomer is an amino acid. Nucleic acids carry instructions — monomer is a nucleotide. Each name, each monomer, each job.

Here is the trap most students fall into. They memorize the name and forget the monomer or the job. The exam will not ask just protein — it asks what protein is built from and what it does. Lock these three together every time. Name, monomer, function. Three columns, one row per macromolecule.

Your turn. Match each macromolecule to its monomer and main function. Carbohydrate, lipid, protein, nucleic acid. Monomers: nucleotide, amino acid, monosaccharide, and the fatty acid plus glycerol pair. Pause now. Write out all four rows. Press play when you are ready.

Time. Carbohydrate, monomer monosaccharide, function quick energy and structure. Lipid, built from fatty acids and glycerol, function long energy storage and membranes. Protein, monomer amino acid, function enzymes, structure, transport. Nucleic acid, monomer nucleotide, function storing and reading the instructions in DNA and RNA. Three columns. Always three.

Cells build and break these polymers two ways. Dehydration synthesis joins two monomers and removes a water molecule — build means lose water. Hydrolysis adds a water molecule to split a polymer apart — break means add water. Same chemistry, opposite direction. The clue is in the name: hydro-lysis literally means water-splitting.

Pause and try. A cell links two amino acids into a short protein and releases one water molecule. Is that dehydration synthesis or hydrolysis? Then: a digestive enzyme uses a water molecule to cut a starch into smaller sugars. Same question. Pause now. Decide build or break, then press play.

Time. Amino acids joining and releasing water — that is dehydration synthesis. Building a polymer. Loses water. Enzyme using water to cut starch into sugars — that is hydrolysis. Breaking a polymer. Adds water. Quick check: if a water molecule is being released, the cell is building. If water is being used up, the cell is breaking.

Recap. Chemistry is not separate from biology — life is chemistry, organized. Bonds hold molecules together. Water's polarity supports almost every life process. Four macromolecules — carbohydrate, lipid, protein, nucleic acid — each paired with its monomer and job. Build with dehydration synthesis, break with hydrolysis. Same water, opposite direction.

How to actually master this module. Read OpenStax Biology Chapter Two — The Chemistry of Life. Run the Khan Academy practice on biological macromolecules. In your Learn Portal dashboard, complete the concept map assignment that links each macromolecule to its monomer and main function. Book an advisor check-in if either pause felt shaky. Next up — Module Two, Cell Structure and Function.
